title: Barycentric MFA (BaMFA)
text: Multi-subject data often contains two kinds structure: patterns shared across subjects (global components) patterns unique individuals (local components). Standard MFA captures global structure discards subject-specific variation. care individual differences — subjects deviate, — need method explicitly models . BaMFA (Barycentric Multiple Factor Analysis) decomposes multi-block data global local components simultaneously. Global components capture consensus structure; local components capture makes subject unique.

title: Barycentric MFA (BaMFA)
text: simulate 6 subjects shared structure (3 global factors) plus subject-specific structure (2 local factors per subject). Fit BaMFA 3 global 2 local components: Mean global scores across subjects. capture consensus structure shared blocks.
code: library(muscal) library(multivarious) library(ggplot2) length(data_list) #> [1] 6 sapply(data_list, dim) #>      Subject_1 Subject_2 Subject_3 Subject_4 Subject_5 Subject_6 #> [1,]        50        50        50        50        50        50 #> [2,]        20        20        20        20        20        20 fit <- bamfa(data_list, k_g = 3, k_l = 2, niter = 20) fit #> Barycentric Multiple Factor Analysis (BaMFA)  #>  #> Model Structure:  #>   Global components (k_g): 3  #>   Local components (k_l requested): 2  #>   Convergence after 20 iterations #>   Local score regularization (lambda_l): 0  #>  #> Block Information:  #>   Block 1 ( Subject_1 ):  #>     Features: 20  #>     Local components retained: 2  #>   Block 2 ( Subject_2 ):  #>     Features: 20  #>     Local components retained: 2  #>   Block 3 ( Subject_3 ):  #>     Features: 20  #>     Local components retained: 2  #>   Block 4 ( Subject_4 ):  #>     Features: 20  #>     Local components retained: 2  #>   Block 5 ( Subject_5 ):  #>     Features: 20  #>     Local components retained: 2  #>   Block 6 ( Subject_6 ):  #>     Features: 20  #>     Local components retained: 2  #>  #> Final reconstruction error (per feature): 0.0733104

title: Barycentric MFA (BaMFA)
text: key idea: BaMFA decomposes block’s data : 𝐗i≈𝐒i𝐁i⊤+𝐔i𝐕i⊤\mathbf{X}_i \approx \mathbf{S}_i \mathbf{B}_i^\top + \mathbf{U}_i \mathbf{V}_i^\top : 𝐒i\mathbf{S}_i global scores — similar across subjects 𝐁i\mathbf{B}_i global loadings 𝐔i\mathbf{U}_i local scores — unique subject ii 𝐕i\mathbf{V}_i local loadings global loadings 𝐁i\mathbf{B}_i encouraged similar across subjects, local loadings 𝐕i\mathbf{V}_i free differ.

text: lambda_l parameter controls regularization local components. default 0 (regularization). Increasing shrinks local components, pushing structure global subspace:
code: # Strong local regularization — most structure forced into global fit_sparse <- bamfa(data_list, k_g = 3, k_l = 2, niter = 20, lambda_l = 1)

title: Barycentric MFA (BaMFA)
text: k_g (global components): Set number shared factors expect. Start number dominant eigenvalues pooled PCA. k_l (local components): Set capture meaningful individual variation. many local components can absorb noise. useful diagnostic: partial factor scores tight (short lines plot), k_g capturing shared structure well. ’re spread , may need global components.

title: Barycentric MFA (BaMFA)
text: BaMFA appropriate : multi-subject multi-condition data blocks want separate shared (global) individual (local) structure Individual differences scientifically interesting, just noise need interpretable per-subject components BaMFA appropriate : care consensus (use mfa() — simpler faster) Blocks different observations (use anchored_mfa()) data covariance matrices (use covstatis())

title: Bilinear Mixed Models for Repeated Connectivity Matrices
text: collection connectivity matrices — one per scan, session, trial — collected multiple subjects different experimental conditions. want answer questions like: connectivity patterns differ across conditions? patterns characterize individual subjects? Can subject-level traits (age, performance) predicted connectivity? Standard approaches either vectorize matrices (losing structure) analyze one edge time (losing multivariate picture). bilinear_mixed() keeps matrix structure intact learning low-rank row column bases, fitting mixed model compressed space.

title: Bilinear Mixed Models for Repeated Connectivity Matrices
text: ’ll simulate small dataset: 8 subjects, scanned 3 conditions, producing 4-seed x 12-ROI connectivity matrices. linear condition effect subject-specific latent traits drive signal. element data_list 4 x 12 matrix: Fit model supervised trait prediction: model learned 2 x 3 compressed core observation, estimated subject scores, recovered condition-effect map, linked latent space subject traits.
code: library(muscal) library(ggplot2) set.seed(42) n_subject <- 8 n_repeat  <- 3 n_seed    <- 4 n_roi     <- 12  subject   <- rep(paste0("S", seq_len(n_subject)), each = n_repeat) condition <- rep(seq_len(n_repeat), times = n_subject) dim(data_list[[1]]) #> [1]  4 12 length(data_list) #> [1] 24 fit <- bilinear_mixed(   data      = data_list,   subject   = subject,   z         = z,   y         = y_traits,   mode      = "seed_axis",   r_seed    = 2,   r_roi     = 3,   k_subject = 2,   lambda_y  = 1.0,   max_iter  = 30 ) fit #> Bilinear Repeated-Measures Mixed Model #>   mode: seed_axis  #>   observations: 24 | subjects: 8 | rows: 4 | cols: 12  #>   ranks (seed, roi): (2, 3) | subject latent K: 2  #>   connectivity type: cross  #>   axis head iterations: 30

title: Bilinear Mixed Models for Repeated Connectivity Matrices
text: bilinear_mixed() decomposes observed matrix XiX_i : Xi≈L(β0+Bzi+Wts())R⊤X_i \approx L\left(\beta_0 + B z_i + W t_{s()}\right) R^\top : bases LL RR learned pooled covariance data, model alternates updating WW, BB, tt, AA regularized alternating least squares (ALS) loop.

title: Bilinear Mixed Models for Repeated Connectivity Matrices
text: mode argument controls model treats row (seed) dimension. important modelling choice, right answer depends whether rows exchangeable observations structured entities.

what: seed_axis: rows as a matrix axis
title: Bilinear Mixed Models for Repeated Connectivity Matrices
text: default common mode. observed matrix XiX_i projected onto bases simultaneously form compressed core vector: mi=vec(L⊤XiR)∈ℝrseed⋅rroim_i = \text{vec}\bigl(L^\top X_i\, R\bigr) \quad \\mathbb{R}^{r_\text{seed} \cdot r_\text{roi}} mixed model fit core vectors: mi=β0+Bzi+Wts()+εim_i = \beta_0 + B\,z_i + W\,t_{s()} + \varepsilon_i Use seed_axis : matrices represent bivariate relationships two sets entities (seeds ROIs, genes conditions, etc.) want model full matrix pattern single observation. get back: axis slot contains seed--ROI effect maps can visualized heatmaps. trait_maps[[k]] full 4 x 12 matrix, design_maps[[j]] shows jj-th design variable modulates connectivity surface.
code: fit_axis <- bilinear_mixed(   data    = data_list,   subject = subject,   z       = z,   mode    = "seed_axis",   r_seed  = 2,   r_roi   = 3,   k_subject = 2,   max_iter  = 30 ) dim(fit_axis$axis$trait_maps[[1]]) #> [1]  4 12 dim(fit_axis$axis$design_maps[[1]]) #> [1]  4 12

title: Bilinear Mixed Models for Repeated Connectivity Matrices
text: mode, row matrix treated separate observation ROI profile. Instead compressing axes, column (ROI) axis projected: uij=Xi[j,⋅]R∈ℝrroiu_{ij} = X_i[j, \cdot]\; R \quad \\mathbb{R}^{r_\text{roi}} jj indexes rows within matrix ii. creates “long-form” dataset n_obs x n_seed rows, dimension r_roi. mixed model : uij=β0+Bdij+Wts()+εiju_{ij} = \beta_0 + B\,d_{ij} + W\,t_{s()} + \varepsilon_{ij} dijd_{ij} design vector can include repeat-level design ziz_i, row-level covariates row_design, interactions. Use seed_repeat : rows represent entities properties (e.g., seed regions spatial coordinates, voxels tissue-type labels), want properties enter covariates. get back: repeat_head slot contains ROI-space vectors rather full matrices. design expanded include row covariates interactions condition: roi_trait_maps[[k]] vector length n_roi showing ROI loads kk-th subject component:
code: row_cov <- data.frame(   position = seq(-1, 1, length.out = n_seed),   hemi     = rep(c(-1, 1), length.out = n_seed) )  fit_rep <- bilinear_mixed(   data       = data_list,   subject    = subject,   z          = z,   row_design = row_cov,   mode       = "seed_repeat",   r_roi      = 3,   k_subject  = 2,   max_iter   = 30 ) fit_rep$repeat_head$design_names #> [1] "z1"          "row_cov1"    "row_cov2"    "z1:row_cov1" "z1:row_cov2" length(fit_rep$repeat_head$roi_trait_maps[[1]]) #> [1] 12

what: both: fit both heads with a shared ROI basis
title: Bilinear Mixed Models for Repeated Connectivity Matrices
text: mode runs analyses using ROI basis RR, giving complementary views: seed_axis head captures full seed--ROI pattern, seed_repeat head reveals row-level covariates modulate ROI profile. Use : want full matrix picture meaningful row covariates. shared ROI basis ensures two heads comparable:
code: fit_both <- bilinear_mixed(   data       = data_list,   subject    = subject,   z          = z,   row_design = row_cov,   mode       = "both",   r_seed     = 2,   r_roi      = 3,   k_subject  = 2,   max_iter   = 30 ) all.equal(fit_both$axis$R, fit_both$repeat_head$R) #> [1] TRUE

title: Bilinear Mixed Models for Repeated Connectivity Matrices
text: single latent component kk footprint three spaces: seed space (rows activates), ROI space (columns activates), trait space (subject-level outcomes predicts). Understanding component means seeing three views together. ’ll use supervised fit section:
code: fit_sup <- bilinear_mixed(   data      = data_list,   subject   = subject,   z         = z,   y         = y_traits,   mode      = "seed_axis",   r_seed    = 2,   r_roi     = 3,   k_subject = 2,   lambda_y  = 1.0,   max_iter  = 30 )

title: Bilinear Mixed Models for Repeated Connectivity Matrices
text: Every component kk defined four objects stored fitted model: loading matrix WW key interpretation. column W[, k] vectorized r_seed x r_roi core, model pre-computes back-projection original space trait_maps:
code: # Bases learned from the data L <- fit_sup$axis$L    # seed basis: n_seed x r_seed R <- fit_sup$axis$R    # ROI basis:  n_roi  x r_roi  # Per-component quantities W <- fit_sup$axis$W              # loading matrix: (r_seed*r_roi) x K t_scores <- fit_sup$axis$t_scores  # subject scores: n_subject x K A <- fit_sup$axis$A              # supervision map: K x n_traits # trait_maps[[k]] = L %*% matrix(W[,k], r_seed, r_roi) %*% t(R) # This is already computed: dim(fit_sup$axis$trait_maps[[1]]) #> [1]  4 12

title: Bilinear Mixed Models for Repeated Connectivity Matrices
text: understand component acts, extract marginal profiles averaging trait map across axis: seed profile tells rows (seeds) involved component; ROI profile tells columns (ROIs) involved. Seed-space profiles: average connectivity contribution seed components 1 (blue) 2 (orange). ROI-space profiles: average connectivity contribution ROI components 1 2.
code: K <- ncol(t_scores) seed_profiles <- matrix(NA, nrow = n_seed, ncol = K) roi_profiles  <- matrix(NA, nrow = n_roi,  ncol = K)  for (k in seq_len(K)) {   tmap <- fit_sup$axis$trait_maps[[k]]   seed_profiles[, k] <- rowMeans(tmap)   # average across ROIs   roi_profiles[, k]  <- colMeans(tmap)   # average across seeds }

Top-left: seeds involved. Top-right: ROIs involved. Bottom-left: full connectivity map. Bottom-right: component predicts traits, subject scores along x-axis. can generate panel component. code produce arbitrary component kk:

Seed profile — which rows matter? barplot(rowMeans(tmap), names.arg = paste0("S", seq_len(nrow(tmap))),         col = "steelblue", main = paste("Comp", k, "- seed profile"))  # 2. ROI profile — which columns matter? barplot(colMeans(tmap), names.arg = paste0("R", seq_len(ncol(tmap))),         col = "darkorange", main = paste("Comp", k, "- ROI profile"))  # 3. Full connectivity map image(seq_len(nrow(tmap)), seq_len(ncol(tmap)), tmap,       col = hcl.colors(64, "RdBu", rev = TRUE),       main = paste("Comp", k, "- connectivity"))  # 4. Trait prediction plot(fit_sup$axis$t_scores[, k], y_traits[, 1], pch = 19,      xlab = paste("Comp", k, "score"), ylab = "Trait",      main = paste("Comp", k, "- traits"))

title: Bilinear Mixed Models for Repeated Connectivity Matrices
text: need best configuration, bilinear_mixed_tune() performs subject-blocked cross-validation grid key parameters: CV reconstruction error candidate. Lower better. best candidate automatically refit full dataset stored tuned$fit. supervised problems trait data, use metric = "trait_r2" select configuration best predicts subject traits:
code: tuned <- bilinear_mixed_tune(   data    = data_list,   subject = subject,   z       = z,   y       = y_traits,   metric  = "reconstruction",   n_folds = 2,   grid    = data.frame(     r_seed    = c(1, 2, 3),     r_roi     = c(2, 3, 4),     k_subject = c(1, 2, 2),     lambda_y  = c(0.5, 1, 1)   ) ) tuned #> Bilinear Mixed Tuning #>   metric: reconstruction  #>   best score: 0.01992  #>   best params: #>    - r_seed = 2  #>    - r_roi = 3  #>    - k_subject = 2  #>    - lambda_y = 1  #>  #> Top candidates: #>  candidate r_seed r_roi k_subject lambda_y      score n_success #>          2      2     3         2      1.0 0.01991592         2 #>          1      1     2         1      0.5 0.02454530         2 #>          3      3     4         2      1.0 0.03535052         2 tuned_trait <- bilinear_mixed_tune(   data    = data_list,   subject = subject,   z       = z,   y       = y_traits,   metric  = "trait_r2" )

title: COVSTATIS Analysis
text: correlation covariance matrices multiple subjects — say, resting-state functional connectivity measured 10 people — want find shared structure. brain regions co-activate consistently across subjects? subjects outliers? COVSTATIS answers questions computing compromise matrix optimally represents set, projecting variables (ROIs) subjects common low-dimensional space. COVSTATIS covariance-matrix variant STATIS, “French school” method analyzing collections data tables.

title: COVSTATIS Analysis
text: simulate 8 subjects, 12 x 12 correlation matrix. subjects share underlying factor structure, individual noise. Fit COVSTATIS extract compromise: ROI scores compromise space. Variables co-vary consistently across subjects cluster together. ROI score plot shows 12 variables positioned according role compromise. Variables close together similar correlation patterns across subjects.
code: library(muscal) library(multivarious) library(ggplot2) # 8 subjects, each with a 12 x 12 correlation matrix length(cor_list) #> [1] 8 dim(cor_list[[1]]) #> [1] 12 12 fit <- covstatis(cor_list, ncomp = 3)

title: COVSTATIS Analysis
text: algorithm four steps: Preprocess matrix — optionally double-center (Gower transformation) normalize unit Frobenius norm. Compute RV matrix — matrix pairwise similarities input matrices (analogous correlations subjects). Determine optimal weights (alpha) first eigenvector RV matrix. Subjects whose matrices “typical” receive higher weight. Form compromise weighted sum normalized matrices, eigendecompose get scores loadings.

title: COVSTATIS Analysis
text: subject contributes differently compromise. alpha weights reveal representative: Block weights (alpha). Higher weight means subject’s matrix representative group. can also view subjects compromise space: Subject scores. Subjects close together similar correlation structure.

title: Integrative PCA (iPCA)
text: run PCA concatenated multi-block data, blocks variables higher variance dominate result. MFA addresses normalizing block’s first singular value 1. single global correction — doesn’t adapt actual signal content block. Integrative PCA (iPCA) takes different approach. applies per-block multiplicative penalties continuously reweight block’s contribution based well aligns emerging shared structure. penalty strength lambda controls aggressively blocks equalized: small lambda approaches standard PCA, large lambda gives block equal influence regardless size. iPCA introduced Tang Allen (2021) implemented using efficient Flip-Flop algorithm.

title: Integrative PCA (iPCA)
text: Fit iPCA moderate penalty: iPCA scores. multiplicative penalty balances block contributions preserving shared structure.
code: library(muscal) library(multivarious) library(ggplot2) sim <- synthetic_multiblock(   S = 4, n = 60,   p = c(20, 30, 15, 25),   r = 3, sigma = 0.3, seed = 42 ) sapply(sim$data_list, dim) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] #> [1,]   60   60   60   60 #> [2,]   20   30   15   25 fit <- ipca(sim$data_list, ncomp = 3, lambda = 1) S <- scores(fit) dim(S) #> [1] 60  3

title: Integrative PCA (iPCA)
text: iPCA solves penalized eigenvalue problem. KK blocks 𝐗1,…,𝐗K\mathbf{X}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{X}_K, finds shared eigenvectors uu maximizing: ∑k=1Kfλ(u⊤𝐗k⊤𝐗kutr(𝐗k⊤𝐗k))\sum_{k=1}^K f_\lambda\!\left(\frac{u^\top \mathbf{X}_k^\top \mathbf{X}_k\, u} {\mathrm{tr}(\mathbf{X}_k^\top \mathbf{X}_k)}\right) fλf_\lambda concave penalty function (multiplicative Frobenius penalty). upweights blocks align well current direction downweights don’t — soft, adaptive form integration.

title: Integrative PCA (iPCA)
text: lambda controls integration strength: lambda near 0: Standard PCA concatenated data (reweighting). Blocks variables dominate. lambda = 1: Moderate integration. Blocks softly equalized. Large lambda (10+): Strong equalization. Every block contributes roughly equally, regardless size. Effect lambda score space. Low lambda (left) lets larger blocks dominate; high lambda (right) equalizes contributions.
code: fit_low  <- ipca(sim$data_list, ncomp = 3, lambda = 0.01) fit_high <- ipca(sim$data_list, ncomp = 3, lambda = 10)

title: Integrative PCA (iPCA)
text: ipca_tune_alpha() selects optimal penalty holding fraction data measuring reconstruction error: Held-MSE across alpha (lambda) values. Lower better. tuning result includes refit full data best alpha:
code: tune <- ipca_tune_alpha(   sim$data_list, ncomp = 3,   alpha_grid = c(0.01, 0.1, 1, 10),   holdout_frac = 0.1 ) tune$best_alpha #> [1] 10 fit_tuned <- tune$fit dim(scores(fit_tuned)) #> [1] 60  3

text: Standard MFA requires blocks share observations. often unrealistic. Consider neuroimaging: reference dataset (Y) structural measures N subjects, auxiliary data — task fMRI, resting-state, behavioral assessments — available overlapping different subsets subjects. subjects completed one scan, others completed two, overlap varies modality. Anchored MFA solves . learns shared score matrix reference observations allowing auxiliary block row structure. simply specify rows auxiliary block correspond rows reference. Observations Anchored MFA shared score space, colored block coverage. Darker points data sources informing position. score plot colors observations many blocks contribute position. Subjects measured across modalities information constraining scores.

title: Multiple Factor Analysis
text: Multiple Factor Analysis (MFA) solves common problem: measured observations using different instruments, different feature sets, different time points — want single, integrated view. Standard PCA concatenated data fails blocks variables higher variance dominate solution. MFA corrects normalizing block integration, every data source contributes fairly. MFA belongs “French school” multivariate analysis, developed Escofier Pagès 1980s. remains one principled approaches multi-table data integration.

title: Penalized Multiple Factor Analysis
text: Standard mfa() normalizes blocks contribute equally, places constraint loadings . block’s loadings can point completely different directions. expect blocks share similar variable structure — say, brain regions measured different conditions — want corresponding variables load similarly across blocks. Penalized MFA adds regularization penalty encourages loading matrices similar, still allowing block express unique structure. lambda parameter controls trade-: lambda = 0 recovers independent per-block PCA; large lambda forces near-identical loadings.

title: Penalized Multiple Factor Analysis
text: Fit Penalized MFA moderate penalty: Consensus scores Penalized MFA. observation positioned mean block-specific scores.
code: library(muscal) library(multivarious) library(ggplot2) sim <- synthetic_multiblock(   S = 4, n = 60, p = 25, r = 3,   sigma = 0.3, seed = 42 ) sapply(sim$data_list, dim) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] #> [1,]   60   60   60   60 #> [2,]   25   25   25   25 fit <- penalized_mfa(   sim$data_list, ncomp = 3,   lambda = 1, max_iter = 20 ) fit

title: Penalized Multiple Factor Analysis
text: Penalized MFA estimates orthonormal loading matrices 𝐕i∈ℝp×k\mathbf{V}_i \\mathbb{R}^{p \times k} block minimizing: ∑=1S∥𝐗i−𝐗i𝐕i𝐕i⊤∥F2+λ⋅Penalty({𝐕i})\sum_{=1}^{S} \|\mathbf{X}_i - \mathbf{X}_i \mathbf{V}_i \mathbf{V}_i^\top\|_F^2 \;+\; \lambda \cdot \text{Penalty}(\{\mathbf{V}_i\}) first term reconstruction error; second encourages similarity among block loadings. optimization uses Riemannian gradient descent Stiefel manifold (space orthonormal matrices).

title: Penalized Multiple Factor Analysis
text: Three penalty formulations available: "projection" (default): Penalizes distance block-specific projection matrices 𝐏i=𝐕i𝐕i⊤\mathbf{P}_i = \mathbf{V}_i \mathbf{V}_i^\top. Rotation-invariant — subspace matters, basis. "pairwise": Penalizes pairwise distances loading matrices directly. Sensitive rotation. "global_mean": Penalizes block’s deviation mean loading matrix.
code: # Projection penalty (default, recommended) fit_proj <- penalized_mfa(sim$data_list, ncomp = 3,                           lambda = 1, penalty_method = "projection")  # Pairwise penalty fit_pair <- penalized_mfa(sim$data_list, ncomp = 3,                           lambda = 1, penalty_method = "pairwise")

title: Penalized Multiple Factor Analysis
text: penalty strength lambda key tuning parameter: lambda = 0: penalty. block gets independent PCA loadings. Small lambda (0.01–0.1): Gentle encouragement toward similar loadings. Moderate lambda (1–10): Strong similarity constraint still allowing block-specific variation. Large lambda (100+): Forces near-identical loadings across blocks. practical approach compare reconstruction error across grid lambda values pick knee curve:
code: lambdas <- c(0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100) fits <- lapply(lambdas, function(lam) {   penalized_mfa(sim$data_list, ncomp = 3, lambda = lam, max_iter = 20) })

title: Penalized Multiple Factor Analysis
text: default, Penalized MFA uses Adam optimizer Stiefel manifold. can also use plain gradient descent:
code: fit_gd <- penalized_mfa(   sim$data_list, ncomp = 3, lambda = 1,   optimizer = "gradient",   learning_rate = 0.001,   max_iter = 50 )

title: Penalized Multiple Factor Analysis
text: Penalized MFA appropriate : multiple blocks variables (matched variables) expect blocks share similar loading structure want control similarity-reconstruction trade-explicitly Standard MFA loadings noisy divergent across blocks Penalized MFA appropriate : Blocks measure entirely different variables (loading penalty meaningless) need classical MFA normalization guarantees (use mfa()) goal block-level outlier detection (use covstatis())

Additional named elements passed via ... include: B_list – block-specific local loading matrices. S_list – block-specific global score matrices. U_list – block-specific local score matrices. k_g – number global components final model. k_l – requested number local components. lambda_l – regularization parameter used local scores U_i. niter_actual – actual number iterations performed. objective_trace – objective function value iteration. g_change_trace – relative change global loadings iteration. data_names – names input blocks/subjects. proclist_fitted – list fitted preprocessors block.

title: Barycentric Multiple Factor Analysis (BaMFA) — bamfa
text: algorithm models data block \(X_i\) : $$X_i = S_i G^T + U_i B_i^T + E_i$$ \(G\) represents shared global loadings, \(B_i\) represents block-specific local loadings, \(S_i\) \(U_i\) corresponding scores, \(E_i\) noise. Loadings constrained orthonormal (\(G^T G = , B_i^T B_i = \)). algorithm aims minimize total reconstruction error: $$\sum_{=1}^{m} ||X_i - S_i G^T - U_i B_i^T||_F^2$$ using iterative alternating optimization strategy (similar Expectation-Maximization): Preprocessing: block preprocessed using provided preproc pipeline. Initialization: Initialize global loadings G (via SVD mean block). Iterate (E-step like): block , holding G fixed, update scores S_i (global projection), calculate residuals, find local basis B_i residuals (via SVD), estimate local scores U_i (via projection, potentially regularized lambda_l). Iterate (M-step like): Holding scores S_i fixed, update global loadings G performing SVD aggregated cross-product matrix sum(X_i^T S_i). Repeat steps 3-4 niter iterations convergence based tol.

title: Barycentric Multiple Factor Analysis (BaMFA) — bamfa
text: Model Choice: Assumes linear factor model orthogonal global local components. Initialization: Results can sensitive initialization G. Local Minima: alternating optimization algorithm may converge local minimum. Interpretation: separation global local components depends model fit ranks (k_g, k_l). Regularization (lambda_l): Penalizes squared Frobenius norm local scores U_i via ridge regression. Inference: method provide p-values confidence intervals.

title: Barycentric Multiple Factor Analysis (BaMFA) — bamfa
code: # Generate example multi-block data (e.g., 3 subjects, 10 conditions, 50 features) set.seed(123) n_obs <- 10 n_features <- 50 n_subjects <- 3 data_list <- lapply(1:n_subjects, function(i) {   matrix(rnorm(n_obs * n_features), n_obs, n_features) +   matrix(rnorm(n_obs * 1, mean=i), n_obs, n_features) # Add subject offset }) names(data_list) <- paste0("Subject_", 1:n_subjects)  # Run BaMFA with k_g=3 global, k_l=2 local components result <- bamfa(data_list, k_g = 3, k_l = 2, niter = 10) print(result) #> Barycentric Multiple Factor Analysis (BaMFA)  #>  #> Model Structure:  #>   Global components (k_g): 3  #>   Local components (k_l requested): 2  #>   Convergence after 10 iterations #>   Local score regularization (lambda_l): 0  #>  #> Block Information:  #>   Block 1 ( Subject_1 ):  #>     Features: 50  #>     Local components retained: 2  #>   Block 2 ( Subject_2 ):  #>     Features: 50  #>     Local components retained: 2  #>   Block 3 ( Subject_3 ):  #>     Features: 50  #>     Local components retained: 2  #>  #> Final reconstruction error (per feature): 0.458139

title: Integrated Principal Components Analysis (generic) — ipca
text: practice, iPCA useful want: - shared sample-space representation across blocks (joint scores), - block-specific feature loadings, - stable integration high-dimensional regimes (`p_k >> n`). Relative methods MCCA/MFA, iPCA often robust block covariance/noise structures differ, can slower always best purely correlation-maximizing objectives. ipca.list() converts list blocks multiblock object dispatches ipca.multiblock(). ipca.multiblock() implements multiplicative Frobenius iPCA Tang Allen (2021) using Flip-Flop algorithm. method = "gram" mode, fitting performed sample space (n x n) avoids p_k x p_k eigendecompositions. exact multiplicative Frobenius updates.

title: Multiblock Canonical Correlation Analysis (generic) — mcca
text: reference implementation package uses ridge-stabilized MAXVAR GCCA formulation remains well-posed blocks variables rows (\(p \gg n\)) rank-deficient preprocessing (e.g., centering). mcca.list method applies MCCA list data matrices data frames. method first converts list multiblock object calls mcca.multiblock(). mcca.multiblock method implements ridge-stabilized MAXVAR generalized CCA. Internally computes compromise score space eigendecomposition weighted sum block projection operators: $$H = \sum_s w_s \, X_s (X_s^T X_s + \kappa_s )^{-1} X_s^T.$$ ridge parameter applied per block \(\kappa_s = \text{ridge}_s \cdot \bar{d}_s\), \(\bar{d}_s\) mean diagonal block Gram matrix \(X_s X_s^T\). scaling makes default ridge behave reasonably across different variable scalings.

Additional arguments passed methods. Currently unused included S3 method consistency. ncomp Integer number latent components extract (default: 2). automatically capped minimum number columns across blocks preprocessing. Must least 1. lambda Non-negative scalar controlling strength penalty (default: 1). Larger values encourage similarity among block loadings. `lambda = 0`, method reduces independent PCA block. Typical range: 0 10. penalty_method Character string specifying penalty type (default: "projection"). Options : "projection": Rotation-invariant penalty projection matrices (recommended) "pairwise": Pairwise Euclidean distances loading matrices "global_mean": Distance mean loading matrix max_iter Maximum number outer block-coordinate descent iterations (default: 10). Typical range: 10 100. iterations allow better convergence increase computation time. nsteps_inner Number gradient update steps per block outer iteration (default: 5). Higher values perform thorough optimization block moving next. Typical range: 1 20. learning_rate Step size optimizer (default: 0.01). Controls far move gradient direction. large may cause divergence; small slows convergence. Typical range gradient descent: 0.001 0.1. Adam less sensitive parameter. optimizer Character string specifying optimization algorithm (default: "adam"). Options : "adam": Adaptive Moment Estimation momentum adaptive learning rates "gradient": Standard gradient descent fixed learning rate Adam generally robust converges faster default settings. preproc preprocessing specification (default: `multivarious::center()`). Can : single `pre_processor` object applied blocks list `pre_processor` objects (one per block) NULL preprocessing (identity transformation) Common options: `center()`, `standardize()`, `pass()` (-op). beta1 Adam hyperparameter first moment decay (default: 0.9). Controls exponential decay rate gradient moving average. Typical range: 0.8 0.95. used `optimizer = "adam"`. beta2 Adam hyperparameter second moment decay (default: 0.999). Controls exponential decay rate squared gradient moving average. Typical range: 0.99 0.9999. used `optimizer = "adam"`. adam_epsilon Small constant added denominator Adam numerical stability (default: 1e-8). Prevents division zero. used `optimizer = "adam"`. tol_obj Numeric tolerance outer loop convergence based relative change objective function (default: 1e-7). Smaller values require precise convergence. Typical range: 1e-8 1e-5. tol_inner Optional numeric tolerance inner loop convergence based Frobenius norm change loading matrix (default: NULL, early stopping). specified, inner loop stops \(\|\mathbf{V}_i^{\text{new}} - \mathbf{V}_i\|_F < \text{tol_inner}\). compute_consensus Logical indicating whether compute consensus loading matrix (default: FALSE). computed blocks number features preprocessing. consensus orthonormalized sum block-specific loadings. verbose Logical indicating whether print iteration progress (default: FALSE). title: Penalized Multiple Factor Analysis (MFA) — penalized_mfa
text: ## Optimization Problem \(S\) data blocks \(\mathbf{X}_i \\mathbb{R}^{n \times p_i}\) (\(= 1, \ldots, S\)), method estimates loading matrices \(\mathbf{V}_i \\mathbb{R}^{p_i \times k}\) orthonormal columns (\(\mathbf{V}_i^\top \mathbf{V}_i = \mathbf{}_k\)) minimizing: $$   \min_{\{V_i\}} \sum_{=1}^S \|\mathbf{X}_i - \mathbf{X}_i \mathbf{V}_i \mathbf{V}_i^\top\|_F^2   \;+\; \lambda \,\text{Penalty}(\{\mathbf{V}_i\}) $$ first term reconstruction error (sum squared residuals) block, second term penalty encourages similarity among loading matrices. ## Penalty Options Three penalty formulations available: Projection (default, rotation-invariant): $$\sum_{=1}^S \|\mathbf{P}_i - \bar{\mathbf{P}}\|_F^2$$     \(\mathbf{P}_i = \mathbf{V}_i \mathbf{V}_i^\top\) projection     matrix block \(\) \(\bar{\mathbf{P}} = \frac{1}{S}\sum_{=1}^S \mathbf{P}_i\).     penalty invariant rotations loading matrices recommended     orientation latent space arbitrary. Global Mean (rotation-invariant): $$\sum_{=1}^S \|\mathbf{V}_i - \bar{\mathbf{V}}\|_F^2$$     \(\bar{\mathbf{V}} = \frac{1}{S}\sum_{=1}^S \mathbf{V}_i\). directly     penalizes Euclidean distance mean loading matrix. Computationally simpler     pairwise rotation-invariant. Pairwise (rotation-invariant): $$\sum_{< j} \|\mathbf{V}_i - \mathbf{V}_j\|_F^2$$     penalizes pairwise Euclidean distances loading matrices.     Equivalent global mean scaling factor conceptually different. ## Optimization Algorithm algorithm uses block-coordinate descent Riemannian gradient descent Stiefel manifold: 1. **Outer loop** (max_iter iterations): Cycles blocks 2. **Inner loop** (nsteps_inner steps per block): Updates block's loading matrix 3. **Gradient computation**: Combines reconstruction gradient penalty gradient 4. **Tangent space projection**: Projects gradient onto Stiefel manifold tangent space 5. **Update step**: Uses either gradient descent Adam optimizer 6. **Retraction**: Re-orthonormalizes via QR decomposition maintain manifold constraint Stiefel manifold constraint ensures \(\mathbf{V}_i^\top \mathbf{V}_i = \mathbf{}_k\) iterations, critical identifiability interpretability. ## Convergence Criteria outer loop stops relative change objective function falls `tol_obj`: $$\frac{|f^{(t+1)} - f^{(t)}|}{|f^{(t)}| + \epsilon} < \text{tol_obj}$$ Optionally, inner loop can stop early Frobenius norm change \(\mathbf{V}_i\) falls `tol_inner`. ## Preprocessing Data preprocessing handled via `multivarious` package. Common preprocessing steps include centering (default) scaling. block can preprocessor, single preprocessor can applied blocks. Preprocessing "learned" input data stored result object later use new data. ## Consensus Loading Matrix `compute_consensus = TRUE` blocks equal feature dimensions, consensus loading matrix computed orthonormalizing sum block-specific loadings: $$\mathbf{V}_{\text{consensus}} = \text{orth}\left(\sum_{=1}^S \mathbf{V}_i\right)$$ provides single "average" loading matrix summarizes common structure.

title: Penalized Multiple Factor Analysis (MFA) — penalized_mfa
text: function supports three input types via S3 methods: list list numeric matrices, representing data block multiblock multiblock object multivarious package multidesign multidesign object subject treated separate block

title: Penalized Multiple Factor Analysis (MFA) — penalized_mfa
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Example 1: Basic usage with simulated data set.seed(123) data_list <- lapply(1:3, function(i) {   matrix(rnorm(100), 10, 10) }) res <- penalized_mfa(data_list, ncomp=2, lambda=1, penalty_method="projection",                      optimizer="adam", max_iter=50, verbose=TRUE) print(res)  # Access block-specific loadings V_list <- attr(res, "V_list") print(V_list[[1]])  # Loadings for first block  # Plot convergence obj_vals <- attr(res, "obj_values") plot(obj_vals, type='b', xlab='Iteration', ylab='Objective',      main='Convergence of Penalized MFA')  # Example 2: With consensus and custom preprocessing library(multivarious) res2 <- penalized_mfa(data_list, ncomp=3, lambda=2,                       preproc=standardize(),  # Center and scale                       compute_consensus=TRUE,                       verbose=FALSE) consensus_loadings <- attr(res2, "consensus")  # Example 3: Comparing penalty methods res_proj <- penalized_mfa(data_list, ncomp=2, lambda=1, penalty_method="projection") res_mean <- penalized_mfa(data_list, ncomp=2, lambda=1, penalty_method="global_mean") res_pair <- penalized_mfa(data_list, ncomp=2, lambda=1, penalty_method="pairwise")  # Example 4: Lambda selection via objective values lambdas <- c(0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10) results <- lapply(lambdas, function(lam) {   fit <- penalized_mfa(data_list, ncomp=2, lambda=lam, verbose=FALSE)   list(lambda=lam, final_obj=tail(attr(fit, "obj_values"), 1)) })  # Example 5: Using with multiblock object mb <- multiblock(data_list) res_mb <- penalized_mfa(mb, ncomp=2, lambda=1)  # Example 6: Different preprocessors per block preproc_list <- list(   center(),   standardize(),   pass()  # No preprocessing for third block ) res_custom <- penalized_mfa(data_list, ncomp=2, lambda=1,                             preproc=preproc_list)  # Example 7: Gradient descent instead of Adam res_gd <- penalized_mfa(data_list, ncomp=2, lambda=1,                         optimizer="gradient",                         learning_rate=0.001,                         max_iter=100) } # }

title: Penalized MFA with Clusterwise Spatial Smoothness Constraints — penalized_mfa_clusterwise
text: function implements spatially-regularized Multiple Factor Analysis multi-subject neuroimaging spatial cluster data. Unlike standard MFA encourages similarity among block loadings globally, method uses spatial coordinates clusters (e.g., brain regions, spatial locations) construct graph enforces smoothness loadings among spatially adjacent clusters via graph Laplacian penalty.

title: Penalized MFA with Clusterwise Spatial Smoothness Constraints — penalized_mfa_clusterwise
text: data_list list length \(S\) containing numeric matrices. element \(\mathbf{X}_s\) \(n_s \times k_s\) matrix \(n_s\) number observations \(k_s\) number clusters/features subject \(s\). Data column-centered (mean zero per cluster) proper reconstruction. coords_list list length \(S\) containing coordinate matrices. element \(k_s \times 3\) matrix (x, y, z) spatial coordinates clusters corresponding data block. Must exactly 3 columns. ncomp Integer number latent components extract per block (default: 2). block fewer `ncomp` clusters, effective rank automatically reduced match number clusters. lambda Non-negative scalar controlling spatial smoothness penalty strength (default: 1). Larger values enforce stronger smoothness among spatially adjacent clusters. \(\lambda = 0\), method reduces independent PCA per block. Typical range: 0.1 10. adjacency_opts named list options passed internal `spatial_constraints` function adjacency matrix construction (default: empty list). Supported options: k_nn: Number nearest neighbors (default: 6) max_iter Maximum number outer block-coordinate descent iterations (default: 10). iterations allow better convergence increase computation time. Typical range: 10 100. nsteps_inner Number gradient update steps per block outer iteration (default: 1). Higher values perform thorough optimization block moving next. stability, starting 1 recommended. learning_rate Step size optimizer (default: 0.01). Controls magnitude updates gradient direction. large may cause divergence; small slows convergence. Typical range: 0.001 0.1. optimizer Character string specifying optimization algorithm (default: "gradient"). Options : "gradient": Standard gradient descent fixed learning rate "adam": Adaptive Moment Estimation momentum adaptive learning rates Adam generally robust gradient descent offers control. beta1 Adam hyperparameter first moment decay (default: 0.9). Controls exponential decay rate gradient moving average. Typical range: 0.8 0.95. used `optimizer = "adam"`. beta2 Adam hyperparameter second moment decay (default: 0.999). Controls exponential decay rate squared gradient moving average. Typical range: 0.99 0.9999. used `optimizer = "adam"`. adam_epsilon Small constant added denominator Adam numerical stability (default: 1e-8). Prevents division zero. used `optimizer = "adam"`. tol_obj Numeric tolerance outer loop convergence based relative change objective function (default: 1e-7). algorithm stops \(|f^{(t+1)} - f^{(t)}| / (|f^{(t)}| + \epsilon) < \text{tol_obj}\). Smaller values require precise convergence. Typical range: 1e-8 1e-5. tol_inner Optional numeric tolerance inner loop convergence based Frobenius norm change loading matrix (default: NULL, early stopping). specified, inner loop stops \(\|\mathbf{V}_s^{\text{new}} - \mathbf{V}_s\|_F < \text{tol_inner}\). verbose Logical indicating whether print iteration progress (default: FALSE). TRUE, displays detailed information objective values, convergence, memory usage, potential issues using `cli` package. preproc Preprocessing specification (default: NULL, force centering). Can : NULL: Automatically applies centering block single `pre_processor` object: Applied independently block list `pre_processor` objects: One per block **Critical:** Preprocessing must preserve number columns maintain spatial correspondence. memory_budget_mb Numeric specifying maximum memory megabytes allocated per block precomputing \(\mathbf{X}_s^\top \mathbf{X}_s\) (default: 1024). block's \(k_s^2 \times 8 / (1024^2)\) exceeds budget, gradients computed --fly instead. Increase better speed large memory; decrease memory constrained. normalized_laplacian Logical indicating whether use normalized graph Laplacian (default: TRUE). TRUE, uses \(\mathbf{L}_{\text{sym}} = \mathbf{D}^{-1/2} \mathbf{L} \mathbf{D}^{-1/2}\) scale-free smoothness independent node degree. FALSE, uses unnormalized Laplacian \(\mathbf{L} = \mathbf{D} - \mathbf{}\). Normalized recommended applications.

title: Penalized MFA with Clusterwise Spatial Smoothness Constraints — penalized_mfa_clusterwise
text: ## Optimization Problem \(S\) subjects/blocks data matrices \(\mathbf{X}_s \\mathbb{R}^{n_s \times k_s}\) (\(k_s\) number clusters subject \(s\)), estimate orthonormal loading matrices \(\mathbf{V}_s \\mathbb{R}^{k_s \times r_s}\) minimizing: $$   \min_{\{\mathbf{V}_s\}} \sum_{s=1}^S \|\mathbf{X}_s - \mathbf{X}_s \mathbf{V}_s \mathbf{V}_s^\top\|_F^2   + \lambda \,\text{tr}(\mathbf{V}^\top \mathbf{L} \mathbf{V}) $$ : first term reconstruction error (sum squared residuals) \(\mathbf{V}\) vertical concatenation \(\mathbf{V}_s\) \(\mathbf{L} = \mathbf{D} - \mathbf{}\) graph Laplacian \(\mathbf{}\) adjacency matrix constructed spatial coordinates \(\mathbf{D}\) degree matrix (\(D_{ii} = \sum_j A_{ij}\)) \(\lambda \geq 0\) controls spatial smoothness strength ## Spatial Smoothness Penalty Laplacian penalty \(\text{tr}(\mathbf{V}^\top \mathbf{L} \mathbf{V})\) can rewritten : $$   \frac{1}{2} \sum_{,j} A_{ij} \|\mathbf{v}_i - \mathbf{v}_j\|_2^2 $$ penalizes squared Euclidean distance loadings adjacent clusters. two clusters \(\) \(j\) connected (\(A_{ij} = 1\)), loading vectors \(\mathbf{v}_i\) \(\mathbf{v}_j\) encouraged similar. **Normalized vs. Unnormalized Laplacian:** Unnormalized (normalized_laplacian = FALSE) \(\mathbf{L} = \mathbf{D} - \mathbf{}\). Smoothness scaled node degree;     high-degree nodes stronger smoothness constraints. Normalized (normalized_laplacian = TRUE, default) \(\mathbf{L}_{\text{sym}} = \mathbf{D}^{-1/2} \mathbf{L} \mathbf{D}^{-1/2}\).     Scale-free smoothness independent node degree. Recommended     applications. ## Graph Construction spatial adjacency graph built using k-nearest neighbors (k-NN) 3D space: Cluster coordinates subjects pooled cluster, find k nearest neighbors (default: k=6) Create edges cluster neighbors Symmetrize adjacency matrix number neighbors can controlled via `adjacency_opts = list(k_nn = k)`. ## Optimization Algorithm method uses block-coordinate descent (BCD) Riemannian optimization: 1. **Initialization**: Initialize \(\mathbf{V}_s\) via SVD \(\mathbf{X}_s\) 2. **Outer loop** (max_iter iterations): Cycle subjects/blocks 3. **Inner loop** (nsteps_inner steps per block):    - Compute reconstruction gradient: \(\nabla_{\mathbf{V}_s} = -2 \mathbf{X}_s^\top \mathbf{X}_s \mathbf{V}_s\)    - Compute spatial gradient: \(2\lambda \mathbf{L}_{ss} \mathbf{V}_s + 2\lambda \mathbf{L}_{s,-s} \mathbf{V}_{-s}\)    - Project combined gradient onto Stiefel manifold tangent space    - Update via gradient descent Adam    - Retract manifold via QR decomposition 4. **Convergence check**: Stop relative change objective < `tol_obj` ## Memory Management large datasets, computing storing \(\mathbf{X}_s^\top \mathbf{X}_s\) blocks may exceed available memory. `memory_budget_mb` parameter controls tradeoff: **Precomputed mode** (\(k_s^2 \times 8 / (1024^2)\) < `memory_budget_mb`):     Store \(\mathbf{X}_s^\top \mathbf{X}_s\) faster gradient computation **--fly mode** (otherwise): Compute gradients needed, saving memory     cost computation time ## Variable-Rank Loadings subjects different numbers clusters, block can different effective rank. algorithm automatically sets \(r_s = \min(\text{ncomp}, k_s)\) block pads concatenated loading matrix zeros consistency. ## Preprocessing Data preprocessing crucial reconstruction-based methods. function defaults centering block preprocessing specified. **Important:** Preprocessing must preserve number columns (clusters) maintain correspondence spatial coordinates. ## Lambda = 0 \(\lambda = 0\), spatial penalty disabled method reduces independent PCA block. provides useful baseline comparison. ## Practical Considerations Choosing lambda Start \(\lambda = 0\) (smoothness) gradually increase.     Typical range: 0.1 10. Monitor objective function components     (reconstruction vs. smoothness) balance tradeoff. Number neighbors neighbors (larger k-NN) create denser graph stronger smoothness     constraints. Default k=6 works well 3D spatial data. Convergence method may converge slowly large \(\lambda\). Increase `max_iter`     adjust `learning_rate` needed. Optimizer choice Adam generally robust converges faster. Use gradient descent     better control Adam's adaptive behavior undesired.

title: Penalized MFA with Clusterwise Spatial Smoothness Constraints — penalized_mfa_clusterwise
text: implementation includes several optimizations: **Mathematical**: Normalized Laplacian default, \(\lambda=0\) optimization skips **Numerical**: Sparse matrix operations, -block QR retraction, fast orthogonalization **Stability**: Variable-rank loadings, robust parameter validation, Adam state management **Architecture**: Modular helper functions, comprehensive logging, clear error messages **Memory**: Adaptive memory budget, efficient gradient computation strategies **API**: Shorter `pmfa_cluster()` alias, consistent naming conventions

title: Penalized MFA with Clusterwise Spatial Smoothness Constraints — penalized_mfa_clusterwise
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Example 1: Basic usage with simulated spatial cluster data set.seed(123) S <- 3  # 3 subjects  # Generate data with spatial structure data_list <- lapply(1:S, function(s) {   n <- 50  # 50 observations   k <- 20  # 20 clusters   matrix(rnorm(n * k), n, k) })  # Generate 3D spatial coordinates for clusters coords_list <- lapply(1:S, function(s) {   matrix(runif(20 * 3, 0, 10), 20, 3)  # Random 3D positions })  # Fit model with spatial smoothness res <- penalized_mfa_clusterwise(   data_list, coords_list,   ncomp = 3,   lambda = 1,   max_iter = 20,   verbose = TRUE ) print(res)  # Plot convergence plot(res$obj_values, type = 'b', xlab = 'Iteration', ylab = 'Objective',      main = 'Convergence of Spatially-Regularized MFA')  # Example 2: Using the shorter alias res2 <- pmfa_cluster(data_list, coords_list, ncomp = 2, lambda = 0.5)  # Example 3: Compare different lambda values lambdas <- c(0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 5) results <- lapply(lambdas, function(lam) {   fit <- pmfa_cluster(data_list, coords_list, ncomp = 2, lambda = lam,                       verbose = FALSE)   list(     lambda = lam,     final_obj = tail(fit$obj_values, 1),     iterations = fit$iterations_run   ) })  # Example 4: Using Adam optimizer for faster convergence res_adam <- pmfa_cluster(   data_list, coords_list,   ncomp = 3,   lambda = 1,   optimizer = "adam",   learning_rate = 0.05,   max_iter = 30,   verbose = TRUE )  # Example 5: Controlling k-NN graph construction res_dense <- pmfa_cluster(   data_list, coords_list,   ncomp = 2,   lambda = 1,   adjacency_opts = list(k_nn = 10),  # More neighbors = denser graph   verbose = TRUE )  # Example 6: Using unnormalized Laplacian res_unnorm <- pmfa_cluster(   data_list, coords_list,   ncomp = 2,   lambda = 1,   normalized_laplacian = FALSE )  # Example 7: Memory-constrained settings # For large datasets, reduce memory budget res_mem <- pmfa_cluster(   data_list, coords_list,   ncomp = 2,   lambda = 1,   memory_budget_mb = 256,  # Limit memory per block   verbose = TRUE ) # Check which blocks used precomputed gradients print(res_mem$precompute_info)  # Example 8: Extracting block-specific loadings V_list <- res$V_list  # Loadings for first subject V1 <- V_list[[1]] dim(V1)  # k_s x ncomp  # Visualize spatial smoothness of first loading vector library(scatterplot3d) scatterplot3d(   coords_list[[1]],   color = rank(V1[, 1]),   main = "Spatial Pattern of First Loading (Subject 1)",   xlab = "X", ylab = "Y", zlab = "Z" )  # Example 9: Examining the spatial penalty contribution # Extract Laplacian and loadings L <- res$Sadj LV <- res$LV v <- res$v  # Compute spatial penalty: tr(V' L V) spatial_penalty <- sum(LV * v) cat("Spatial penalty term:", spatial_penalty, "\n")  # Example 10: Variable-rank loadings (blocks with different sizes) # Simulate data with varying cluster numbers data_var <- list(   matrix(rnorm(50 * 15), 50, 15),  # 15 clusters   matrix(rnorm(50 * 20), 50, 20),  # 20 clusters   matrix(rnorm(50 * 10), 50, 10)   # 10 clusters )  coords_var <- list(   matrix(runif(15 * 3), 15, 3),   matrix(runif(20 * 3), 20, 3),   matrix(runif(10 * 3), 10, 3) )  res_var <- pmfa_cluster(   data_var, coords_var,   ncomp = 8,  # Will be capped at 10 (smallest block)   lambda = 1,   verbose = TRUE ) # Check effective ranks per block print(res_var$ncomp_block)  # Example 11: Custom preprocessing per block library(multivarious)  preproc_list <- list(   center(),        # Just center first block   standardize(),   # Center and scale second block   center()         # Just center third block )  res_preproc <- pmfa_cluster(   data_list, coords_list,   ncomp = 2,   lambda = 1,   preproc = preproc_list )  # Example 12: Lambda selection via cross-validation style approach # (Simplified - would need proper CV in practice) lambda_grid <- 10^seq(-1, 1, length.out = 10) cv_results <- data.frame(   lambda = lambda_grid,   recon_error = NA,   spatial_penalty = NA,   total_obj = NA )  for (i in seq_along(lambda_grid)) {   fit <- pmfa_cluster(data_list, coords_list, ncomp = 2,                       lambda = lambda_grid[i], verbose = FALSE)   cv_results$total_obj[i] <- tail(fit$obj_values, 1)   # Could decompose into reconstruction and spatial components }  # Plot objective vs lambda plot(cv_results$lambda, cv_results$total_obj, log = "x",      type = "b", xlab = "Lambda (log scale)", ylab = "Final Objective",      main = "Objective Function vs. Smoothness Penalty") } # }

title: Project a Subject-Level Covariate (generic) — project_covariate
code: # Create a list of correlation matrices Xs <- lapply(1:5, function(i) matrix(rnorm(10*10), 10, 10)) Xs <- lapply(Xs, cor)  # Apply COVSTATIS res <- covstatis(Xs, ncomp=3)  # Create a random covariate vector (e.g., episodic memory scores) y <- rnorm(length(Xs))  # Project the covariate to get dimension-wise coordinates dim_cos <- project_covariate(res, y, what = "dimension", scale = "cosine")  # Project the covariate to get an ROI-wise pattern roi_beta <- project_covariate(res, y, what = "observation", scale = "beta")

title: Identify Significant Components via RMT and Inter-block Coherence Tests — significant_components
text: fit fitted multi-block model object containing minimum `V_list` attribute (list loading matrices) optionally `sdev` (singular values). n Integer; number observations (rows) used fit model. k_vec Optional integer vector block dimensions (number columns per block). NULL, inferred `fit` via `block_indices` `V_list`. alpha Numeric; significance level hypothesis tests (default 0.05). check_rmt Logical; TRUE (default), performs Marchenko-Pastur edge test check eigenvalues exceed noise threshold. tail_frac Numeric; fraction smallest eigenvalues used estimate noise variance RMT test (default 0.3).

title: Identify Significant Components via RMT and Inter-block Coherence Tests — significant_components
text: list following elements: keep Integer vector component indices pass tests. rmt_pass Logical vector indicating components pass RMT test. icc_pass Logical vector indicating components pass ICC test. icc Numeric vector inter-block coherence values per component. icc_pvalue Numeric vector p-values ICC test. mp_edge Marchenko-Pastur edge threshold (NA RMT skipped). sigma2_est Estimated noise variance (NA RMT skipped). lambda Eigenvalues (squared singular values). n, k_vec, alpha Input parameters echoed back.

title: Identify Significant Components via RMT and Inter-block Coherence Tests — significant_components
text: RMT test uses Marchenko-Pastur distribution identify eigenvalues exceed expected bulk edge null hypothesis pure noise. Noise variance estimated robustly tail eigenvalue distribution. ICC test measures squared cosine similarity loading vectors across pairs blocks. null hypothesis random loadings, expected value 1/k (k harmonic mean block dimensions). z-test Bonferroni correction applied.
